---
title: "Week 08 — The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis"
format: html
---

Week: 8

Topic: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis

# Week Overview

| Session | Content |
|---------|---------|
| **Session A** | Instructor-led philosophy deep-dive (~75 min) |
| **Session B** | Workshop — project progress, peer review & method justification (~75 min) |

::: {.callout-note}
No student presenter is available this week. Dr. Molina will lead Session A as an instructor-led discussion.
:::

## Learning Objectives

1. Connect the philosophical ideas from Weeks 2, 3, and 5 to your own analysis
2. Articulate and defend your method choice in writing and verbally
3. Identify common philosophical pitfalls in applied statistics
4. Assess your project's current progress against your planned timeline

---

# Session A: The Philosophy Behind Your Analysis (75 min)

## Part 1: Opening — Pulse Check & Minute Paper (10 min)

::: {.callout-tip}
## SoTL Activity #1: Minute Paper

Take an index card and respond to both prompts in 3–4 minutes:

1. **"What is the single most important thing you learned in this course so far?"**

<br><br>

2. **"What is the muddiest point — the thing you still don't understand or feel uncertain about?"**

<br><br><br>
:::

Dr. Molina will collect cards, skim quickly, and address the muddiest points during the mini-lecture.

---

## Part 2: Mini-Lecture — Why Philosophy Matters for YOUR Analysis (20 min)

### Recap: Key Philosophical Ideas from Previous Weeks

| Week | Idea | Implication for Your Work |
|------|------|--------------------------|
| Week 2 | "All models are wrong, some are useful" (Box) | Your model doesn't need to be *true* — it needs to be *defensible* |
| Week 2 | Bayesian vs. frequentist frameworks | Know which framework you're working in **and why** |
| Week 3 | Method–Question–Data Triangle | If these three don't align, your results are unreliable |
| Week 5 | Split-plot, mixed models vs. ANOVA | Same data can produce multiple analyses; your **design** dictates the right one |

### Central Question

> **"Can you defend your analysis to a skeptical reviewer?"**

### Four Requirements of a Good Defense

1. **Philosophical grounding** — What framework are you working in? What assumptions does it require?
2. **Data–method alignment** — Does your method match your response variable type and data structure?
3. **Simplicity vs. complexity** — Is your model as simple as possible while still being honest?
4. **Transparency** — Could someone reproduce your analysis and understand your reasoning?

### Three Common Pitfalls

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pitfall 1: Method Chosen by Habit, Not Design

> "I'm using ANOVA because my advisor always uses ANOVA."

Your design may require a GLMM. Habit is not a philosophical defense.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pitfall 2: Philosophical Mismatch

> Using a Bayesian framework but interpreting results with p-values and null hypothesis language.

Know your framework. Bayesian credible intervals and posterior probabilities are **not** the same as p-values and confidence intervals.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Pitfall 3: The Triangle Is Broken

> Repeated measures + count data, but using a simple t-test.

Check all three corners of the triangle — question, data type, and method — every time.
:::

### Decision Flowchart: Choosing Your Method

```
What is your research question?
        |
        |-----> Descriptive / exploratory?
        |               --> Summary statistics, visualization
        |
        |-----> Predictive?
        |               --> Consider cross-validation, predictive modeling
        |
        |-----> Inferential / causal?
                        |
                        |--> What is your response variable?
                        |
                        |-----> Continuous, normal?
                        |               --> lm(), lmer() if structure present
                        |
                        |-----> Counts (integers >= 0)?
                        |               --> glm(Poisson) or glmer(Poisson)
                        |
                        |-----> Proportions / binary?
                        |               --> glm(binomial) or glmer(binomial)
                        |
                        |--> What is your data structure?
                                |
                                |-----> Simple, independent observations?
                                |               --> lm() or glm()
                                |
                                |-----> Blocked / repeated / nested?
                                                --> lmer() or glmer()
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Key Takeaway

**The best analysis is the simplest one that honestly addresses your question given your data structure.**

Complexity must be *earned* by your data and your question — not borrowed from habit or perceived sophistication.
:::

---

## Part 3: 🤝 Think-Pair-Share — Defend Your Method (20 min)

::: {.callout-tip}
## SoTL Activity #2: Structured Peer Dialogue
:::

### Think (5 min)

Fill out a **Method Defense Card** on a notecard or piece of paper:

| Prompt | Your Response |
|--------|--------------|
| My research question is: | |
| My response variable type is: | |
| My planned analysis is: | |
| Why it fits (reference the Triangle): | |
| One alternative I considered and rejected: | |

### Pair (8 min)

Partner with someone from **outside** your immediate research area.

- Take turns presenting your Method Defense Card (~3 min each)
- Your partner plays the role of **"skeptical reviewer"** and asks one tough question

> Tough question examples: "Why not use a simpler model?" / "How do you know your data meet that assumption?" / "What would a Bayesian say about this?"

### Share (7 min)

3–4 pairs share the toughest question they received. Dr. Molina moderates and connects questions back to the lecture themes.

---

## Part 4: Philosophical Stations — Small Group Rotation (15 min)

Work in groups of 3–4. ⏱ 5 minutes per station, then rotate.

### Station 1 — "The Box Station"

> How is your model "wrong"? Why is it still *useful*?

Write your answer on the station sheet. Be specific — what assumptions does your model violate? Why does it still give you defensible insight?

### Station 2 — "The Inference Station"

> Is your claim **causal**, **predictive**, or **descriptive**?
> Does your analysis actually support that type of claim?

Write the type of inference you are making and one sentence explaining how your method supports (or fails to support) it.

### Station 3 — "The Reproducibility Station"

> Could someone reproduce your analysis from your GitHub repo **today**?

List two things that are currently missing (data, code, README, environment details, etc.).

**Brief whole-class debrief (2–3 min):** What patterns did you notice across stations?

---

## Part 5: Exit Ticket — Self-Assessment (10 min)

::: {.callout-tip}
## SoTL Activity #3: Reflective Self-Assessment

Please respond to all prompts honestly. This is for your own growth and for course improvement.

**1. How confident are you right now in defending your analysis choice?**

⬜ 1 — Not at all confident  
⬜ 2 — Slightly confident  
⬜ 3 — Moderately confident  
⬜ 4 — Quite confident  
⬜ 5 — Very confident

**2. Name one thing you will change or clarify about your project after today:**

<br><br>

**3. Which philosophical idea is most relevant to your project? (check all that apply)**

⬜ "All models are wrong, some are useful"  
⬜ Bayesian vs. frequentist framework  
⬜ Method–Question–Data Triangle  
⬜ Design dictates analysis  
⬜ Simplicity vs. complexity tradeoff

**4. One remaining question I have:**

<br><br>
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Instructor Note: SoTL Data Collection

- **Minute papers** (Part 1) and **exit tickets** (Part 5) can be analyzed qualitatively (theme coding) or quantitatively (confidence ratings over time).
- **Method Defense Cards** (Part 3) can be coded for reasoning quality: Does the student reference the triangle? Do they identify a genuine alternative?
- **Recommendation:** Repeat the confidence rating question (Exit Ticket prompt 1) at Week 10 and Week 14 to track growth across the semester.
:::

---

## Session A Summary

| SoTL Activity | Purpose | Time |
|---------------|---------|------|
| #1 Minute Paper | Assess prior learning; surface muddiest points | 10 min |
| #2 Structured Peer Dialogue (Think-Pair-Share) | Practice verbal defense; expose reasoning gaps through peer challenge | 20 min |
| #3 Reflective Self-Assessment (Exit Ticket) | Track confidence; generate actionable next step; collect longitudinal data | 10 min |
